W1.action_text: One time I weirded out a nice guy who gave me his number because I kept obsessing about the number itself. W1.target: Are you kidding?! That's AMAZING
W1.action_text: THANK YOU. I was so proud to show him. The area code was the short leg. The next three were the long leg. The final four numbers were the hypotenuse. All nice and square. He feigned being impressed and rolled his eyes. I thought (hoped?) he was playing. Oh well.

W1.target: Can you still remember the number tho? I’d say that was cool.
W1.action_text: Thanks. He was not impressed, though. I do not remember the number. But it was very, very square.

W3.target: 2 3/4 years, which I know isn't really that long, but it certainly feels it, especially the older I get.
W3.action_text: True, but it can take a while to find your people, especially if you're struggling with self esteem issues. Apps are nice for making easy connections but they can be dehumanizing. It definitely helps to get out locally too. :-)

W3.target: Yeah, I'm okay if a local LGBT group and that's been a lifesaver and I've made some good friends through it, including people who, for the first time in my life, I don't doubt if they actually like me, but I don't see them that often and that's largely on me. Object permanence etc.
W3.action_text: That's good! Are there any gay gaming groups near you too? Those are usually pretty active communities.

W3.target: No. It's a pretty small town so modt of the more specific LGBT groups are in the cities, at least half an hour (and a small fortune) away by train.
W3.action_text: Ah, that makes it tough. :-( You might have some luck with online gay gaming communities (I'll search myself a little later). It's not quite what you're looking for, but you never know what you might find! And you'd be less at the mercy of the algorithm haha.

W3.target: Yeah, I've recently got more involved in a UK Gaymer Facebook group. Difficult to actually get to know anyone in it, though, especially if, like me, you're not a big competitive multiplayer type.
W3.action_text: Yeah, I'm not into competitive shooters myself, which is limiting. Do they have a group for Among Us? Or Fall Guys?

W3.target: Yeah, I've recently got more involved in a UK Gaymer Facebook group. Difficult to actually get to know anyone in it, though, especially if, like me, you're not a big competitive multiplayer type.

The Moon rotates so it always shows Earth (almost) the same face - albeit in different illuminations! The first image we have of the farside of the Moon is this one from the Soviet Probe, Luna 3 in 1959. 🔭🧪👩🏻‍🔬

The Moon is an excellent (arguably the best) unaided eye target, and has been observed by cultures all over the world for thousands of years. In the west, stories of the “Man” or “Face” in the Moon abound, as illustrated in many story books - this one from around 1720. 🔭🧪👩🏻‍🔬

W1.action_text: What frustrates me about my conservative coworker is her adamance that she’s not a bigot. But anytime she encounters a person who speaks English as a 2nd language, who doesn’t conform to her expectations of gender, or who dyed their hair(?!) she’s so hateful afterward.

W1.target: I’ll admit, I’m terrible about this. I’d immediately be like “why do you care about ______ so much?” to EVERYTHING. Until she understood I was not her audience. Maybe she can’t be changed, but she doesn’t need to be heard.
W1.action_text: I’ve rebuffed her remarks in the past and tried to respond in ways that make it clear I do not share her viewpoints without starting “work drama” but I’ve probably been too subtle. I also worry about how eager she seems for confrontation, to validate her sense of being censored/victimized.

W1.target: Fuck that noise. My favorite thing to do with this kind of person is start making them question their behaviors “Why don’t you like that person with blue hair? Why do you feel that way?” And just keep bringing it forward until they admit either a prejudice, an Aha moment, or at worst writing it off
W1.action_text: With a friend I’d be willing to have that conversation and do that work. I’d have the space and privacy to handle it with care. With this coworker… she’d see it as attack and double-down. I do think some of our department is ready to take this to HR, but I don’t know if that would even help either.

W3.target: Horror isn't a universal. I remember seeing The Exorcist and being confused that people claimed it was the scariest movie they ever saw. I didn't grow up with a strong religious tradition, nor am I Catholic. It didn't work for me, but I can see why it would affect other people.
W3.action_text: This! It can be so subjective. On some level, a lot of horror touches everyone, but other aspects of horror don’t resonate as strongly (or at all) or takes some effort to grasp the full horror of. Now I’m reminded of some of the reactions I heard to Jordan Peele’s “Get Out.” That was… oof.
